title: Hamilton finds rhythm in Ferrari tyre programme as gap to Leclerc narrows
body:
Lewis Hamilton ended the latest session in P6, a modest result on paper, but the real story for Ferrari lies in what the Scuderia achieved behind closed doors with its tyre development programme. With 41 championship points to his name and sitting P4 overall, Hamilton is finally settling into his Ferrari machineryâ€”and the tyre work being conducted away from the headline times suggests the partnership is moving in the right direction.

Charles Leclerc's consistency has been the foundation of Ferrari's season so far. Three consecutive podiumsâ€”P3 in Australia, P3 in China, and P3 in Japanâ€”have him locked in at 49 points and P3 in the championship. That 8-point buffer over Hamilton should concern the Maranello outfit, yet Hamilton's trajectory through the opening three rounds offers encouragement. His opening three rounds have been a building process, with each race extracting more from the Ferrari package. The trend is upward.

The tyre test work specifically targets the compound management that has eluded Hamilton in the early races. Through R1 to R3, Ferrari split its strategy focusâ€”Leclerc adapted quickly to the softer approach needed for qualifying and opening stints, while Hamilton was slower to unlock the aggressive window. That gap is narrowing. The session P6 placement, while not headline-grabbing alongside Kimi Antonelli's P1 dominance at Mercedes and Oscar Piastri's P2 for McLaren, masks Hamilton's real mission: learning Ferrari's tyre philosophy in detail.

Context matters. Kimi Antonelli now leads the championship with 72 pointsâ€”he won in China and Japan back-to-back and finished P2 in Australia. George Russell's P4 in Japan and P2 in China have him at 63 points and P2 overall. Those numbers show how tight the fight is at the front. Hamilton's 41 points put him in the thick of a four-way tussle for third place, with Leclerc holding the edge only by virtue of three pods and nothing lower than P3. One poor raceâ€”a retirement, a qualifying botchâ€”could flip that dynamic entirely.

Ferrari's tyre programme is specifically addressing brake temperature management and mid-corner stability, two areas where Hamilton has been marginally adrift. The session work allowed the team to gather data on the 2026 Pirelli compounds under race-simulation conditions. Hamilton's P6 was partly experimental; the real measure will come when he locks in a clean qualifying lap and a race run where tyre management is no longer a reactive problem but a solved one.

With only three races complete and 19 rounds remaining, Hamilton has time to close that 8-point gap to Leclerc. The tyre testing is the machinery that will get him there. Miami in late April offers the first opportunity to show whether Ferrari's work translates to qualifying pace and race execution.
excerpt: Lewis Hamilton finished P6 in the latest session, but Ferrari's focused tyre testing is showing signs of closing the 8-point gap to Charles Leclerc in the championship standings.
